title: 2024 Likely The Warmest Year On Record - Why It Matters To You
description: 2024 is likely to surpass 2023 as the warmest year on record. Why that matters to your kitchen table issues.
bodyText: ByMarshall Shepherd, Senior Contributor. We likely just lived through the warmest year on record. If that sounds familiar, it is because 2023 currently holds the title. Candidly, I cut back on writing about the “warmest this” or “warmest that” because it is our reality going forward. That story could be written almost monthly at this point. Here’s what concerns me about the fact that 2024 is virtually certain to be the warmest year on record. The climate is sending us a blaring siren sounds and flashing lights. It is resounding. In November, the World Meteorological Organization issued a red alert about the rate of climate change over such a relatively short period of time. A WMO press release said, “The global mean temperature in 2024 is on track to outstrip the temperature even of 2023, the current warmest year. For 16 consecutive months (June 2023 to September 2024), the global mean temperature likely exceeded anything recorded before, and often by a wide margin.” Multiple datasets confirmed this finding. Of significant concern, for much of the year (January to September), global mean temperatures exceeded 1.5 °C (2.7 °F) above the pre-industrial average. Exceeding that threshold is alarming. The press release cautioned, “One or more individual years exceeding 1.5°C does not necessarily mean that ‘pursuing efforts to limit the temperature increase to 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels’ as stated in the Paris Agreement is out of reach.” However, consecutive record hot years should get our attention. WMO data also indicate that the period covering 2015 to 2024 will also be the warmest span of ten years on record. I am always hesitant to write this type of article because atmospheric global warming is just one aspect of climate change. Ocean temperatures are also warming rapidly. In fact, roughly 90% of the warming in the Earth system is in the oceans. The WMO warned, “From 2005 to 2023, the ocean absorbed on average approximately 3.1 million terawatt-hours (TWh) of heat each year.” As a reference point, that is “more than 18 times the world’s energy consumption in 2023,” according to the organization. Climate warming impacts hurricanes, ocean circulations, sea level rise and more. However, the “So What?” goes well beyond those things. Raise your hand if you eat rice. Recent studies reveal that rice yields are adversely affected by temperature and precipitation changes. Ok, maybe you don’t eat rice. What about eggs? Since the price of eggs are cited as a big concern these days, I should point out that a 2024 study found that the poultry industry is particularly vulnerable to climate change. Expected impacts include declining egg production, changes in feed efficiency, and an increase the mortality rate in broilers. The Poultry Site website pointed out, “Animal agriculture, including poultry farming, is particularly susceptible to the adverse effects of climate change. Increased extreme weather events, farm fires facilitated by drought, thermal pressure on farmed animals, reduced availability or increased prices of water, raw materials, and electricity, and much more are already impacting the industry.” I focused on eggs and rice because they go on the kitchen table. It is an attempt to counter narratives that climate change does not affect “everyday life issues.” The DNA of climate change is in contemporary extreme weather events, and our societal well-being is at stake. Hurricane Helene caused over $5 billion in timber and agricultural losses here in Georgia and disrupted infrastructure throughout the Southeast. Earlier this year, massive flooding killed over 200 people in Spain. I could write an encyclopedia of the ways climate warming is affecting our lives. And that is concerning. We have our work cut out for us in the coming years as climate change accelerates and the impacts become increasingly obvious. As with the COVID response, we are in “all hands-on deck” mode. That deck consists of every level of government, varied political perspectives, industry, faith communities, the military, community organizations, and every single person. We talk about plan B. There is no planet B.
